items.text: First-order thinking stops at the obvious: 'profits are up, buy.' Second-order asks what happens next — do rivals now attack harder, does management get complacent, does the crowd already know this so it's priced in? Howard Marks' version: 'It's a good company — but everyone knows that, so it's expensive. Sell.' The money is made by seeing one step past the obvious.

The most useful two words in analysis: 'and then?' Poor monsoon → food prices rise → the central bank hikes interest rates → people spend less on non-essentials → a retailer's sales dip three quarters later. Markets price the first link fast and the later links slowly. Your edge lives two or three links down that chain, where others stop looking.

Munger: 'Show me the incentive and I'll show you the outcome.' If a boss's bonus depends on sales growth, expect them to chase growth — even bad growth. If a company suddenly starts reporting a flattering new profit figure, ask what the old, plainer number would have shown. People quietly bend toward whatever they're rewarded for. So always check what's being rewarded.
'Zomato turned profitable, bears proven wrong, stock will keep rising.' Which is the strongest SECOND-order question?
options: Was the profit bigger than consensus?
How does profitability change what Swiggy, Amazon and the new shareholders do next?
items.explanation: Second-order thinking asks how the event changes other actors' behaviour — competitors, capital, incentives — not just what the event was.

items.text: A government cuts fuel taxes. First-order: airlines profit. A second-order effect to check?
options: Competitors add capacity/cut fares, competing away much of the gain
items.explanation: In competitive, commoditised industries, input-cost windfalls are usually competed away through fares. The structure of competition eats the first-order gain.

items.text: A management team's bonuses vest on revenue growth, not returns on capital. What behaviour should you EXPECT over time?
options: Growth at any price — acquisitions and capex even at poor returns
items.explanation: People optimise what they're paid for. Revenue-linked pay reliably produces empire-building; check the incentive structure before trusting the strategy.

items.text: Asian Paints long earned fat margins in a near-monopoly. In 2024 a deep-pocketed rival, Birla Opus, entered. First-order reaction: 'new competition, sell.' Your task on screener.in: check Asian Paints' operating margin (OPM %) over the last 2-3 years — is there real damage yet, or is the market pricing fear ahead of the facts? Then ask the second-order question: what could the incumbent do that a new entrant can't easily counter? (~5 min)
